coredata.dc:title: Structuring graphene quantum dots anchored CuO for high-performance hybrid supercapacitors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Khalafallah, Diab
Miao, Jizhong
Zhi, Mingjia
Hong, Zhanglian
coredata.dc:description: Pseudocapacitive materials with multiple oxidation/reduction modes can deliver an improved specific capacity and energy density owing to their reversible Faradic redox reactions. Also, ingeniously integrating a conductive carbon material and a pseudocapacitive transition metal to develop reactive compounds with a high conductivity is a plausible solution to make up for the deficiencies of individual oxides and hydroxides. In this study, we rationally design graphene quantum dots decorated CuO nanoframework (GQDs/CuO) for high-performance supercapacitors. Specifically, the Cu-MOF template as the precursor with a confined porosity and skeleton as well as Cu2+ as the central atoms is converted into CuO by in-situ growth and annealing. Afterward, the negatively charged GQDs can adsorb and uniformly anchor on the CuO surface via electrostatic and coordination interactions by a subsequent hydrothermal treatment. The deposited GQDs with carboxyl functional groups not only increase the surface area for electrochemical reactions but also modulate the conductivity and reduce interfacial resistance by allowing effective paths for electron transportation, leading to better redox reaction kinetics. As a supercapacitor cathode material, the integrated GQDs/CuO electrode affords a high specific capacitance of 729 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g–1 and good-rate capability together with an improved cyclic performance (82.2% retention after 3000 cycles). Moreover, the as-fabricated asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) with a large output voltage window of 1.5 V realizes an energy density of 32.2 W h kg–1 at power density of 748.9 W kg–1 with an enhanced cyclability over 8000 cycles, benefiting from the enriched electrochemical active sites and intimated connection between Cu species and doped GQDs. Thus, the adopted strategy may demonstrate an effective opportunity to explore high-performance electrode materials for energy harvesting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs/CuO composite
Porous architectures
Electron/mass transfer
Specific capacity
Asymmetric supercapacitors

Electrochemical properties of the GQDS/CuO composite
Capacitive performance of GQDS/CuO//AC ASC device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TGA curve of the Cu-BTC framework and (b) XRD spectra of CuO and GQDs/CuO samples.
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs of the as-prepared materials, (a-b) structured CuO and (c-d) GQDs/CuO composite.
(a-b) TEM micrographs of the resultant GQDs/CuO composite. (d) STEM image and corresponding EDS profiles of the existing Cu, O, and C elements.
(a) XPS survey spectrum for the GQDs/CuO composite and corresponding high-resolution scans of (b) Cu 2p, (c) C 1s, and (d) O 1s core-levels.

Electrochemical properties of the as-prepared CuO, hydrothermally treated CuO, and GQDs/CuO electrodes in 3 M KOH at room temperature. (a) CVs at 10 mVs−1, (b) CV plots of the GQDs/CuO electrode at various sweep rates, (c) GCD curves of electrodes, (d) GCD curves of the GQDs/CuO electrode at different current densities, (e) plots of specific capacity versus current density and (f) cycling stability of electrodes.

Electrochemical performance of the established GQDs/CuO//AC ASC device. (a) CV profiles for the GQDs/CuO and AC/NF electrodes in a 3 M KOH supporting electrolyte at 10 mV s-1 with a three-electrode system (b) CV curves at various scanning rates, (c) GCD plots at various current densities, (d) plot of specific capacity versus current density, (e) Ragone plot, and (f) cycling stability test.

head.ce:title: Structuring graphene quantum dots anchored CuO for high-performance hybrid supercapacitors

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Silicon Material, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University, 38 Zheda Road, Hangzhou 310027, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicon Material
School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pseudocapacitive materials with multiple oxidation/reduction modes can deliver an improved specific capacity and energy density owing to their reversible Faradic redox reactions. Also, ingeniously integrating a conductive carbon material and a pseudocapacitive transition metal to develop reactive compounds with a high conductivity is a plausible solution to make up for the deficiencies of individual oxides and hydroxides. In this study, we rationally design graphene quantum dots decorated CuO nanoframework (GQDs/CuO) for high-performance supercapacitors. Specifically, the Cu-MOF template as the precursor with a confined porosity and skeleton as well as Cu2+ as the central atoms is converted into CuO by in-situ growth and annealing. Afterward, the negatively charged GQDs can adsorb and uniformly anchor on the CuO surface via electrostatic and coordination interactions by a subsequent hydrothermal treatment. The deposited GQDs with carboxyl functional groups not only increase the surface area for electrochemical reactions but also modulate the conductivity and reduce interfacial resistance by allowing effective paths for electron transportation, leading to better redox reaction kinetics. As a supercapacitor cathode material, the integrated GQDs/CuO electrode affords a high specific capacitance of 729 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g–1 and good-rate capability together with an improved cyclic performance (82.2% retention after 3000 cycles). Moreover, the as-fabricated asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) with a large output voltage window of 1.5 V realizes an energy density of 32.2 W h kg–1 at power density of 748.9 W kg–1 with an enhanced cyclability over 8000 cycles, benefiting from the enriched electrochemical active sites and intimated connection between Cu species and doped GQDs. Thus, the adopted strategy may demonstrate an effective opportunity to explore high-performance electrode materials for energy harvesting.

GQDs/CuO composite is prepared by a solvent evaporation process and hydrothermal reaction. As a supercapacitor cathode material, the composite exhibits a high specific capacitance (729 F g-1 at 1 A g-1) and a good rate capability. Besides, the as-assembled GQDs/CuO//AC ASC device with an optimized voltage window of 1.5 V delivers a suitable energy density (32.2 Wh kg−1) at a power density of 748.9 W kg−1 with a reasonable cycle life over 8000 charge/discharge cycles.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, researchers are exploring variable sustainable energy conversion and storage systems such as fuel cells, batteries, and electrochemical capacitors [1,2]. In this regard, a significant interest has been offered to supercapacitors owing to their fast charging-discharging rates, favorable reversibility, extended lifespan, improved specific energy/power density, and less maintenance costs compared with fuel cells and batteries [1,2]. Hybrid supercapacitors constructed by two asymmetric electrode materials including a battery-type/pseudocapacitive component to contribute energy and an electrical double layer component as a power source are able to fit the features of batteries and capacitors with enhanced performances [3,4]. Along with this, constructing an asymmetric supercapacitor cell in an aqueous electrolyte can greatly enlarge the working voltage and promote capacitance as well as energy density [5,6]. Therefore, it is desirable to design inexpensive and environmentally friendly electrode materials with a high electrochemical activity.

First row transition metal oxides (i.e. Ni, Co, Cu, Mn, and Fe) have been gained a significant interest as proper candidates for a wide range of energy related applications because of their large natural abundance, low preparation cost, non-toxicity, and high availability of active sites for electrochemical redox reactions [7-12]. Consequently, these electrodes displayed a good capability to store charges and proven to be an effective approach for boosting the electrochemical performance of pseudocapacitors because of their rapid reversible Faradic reactions [13-16]. In particular, copper-based oxides represent an interesting class of pseudocapacitive materials with good theoretical capacitances [17,18]. For example, Wang et al. synthesized free-standing nanosheet arrays like CuO and showed a specific capacity of 569 F g−1 at a current density of 5 mA cm−2 [19]. Mesoporous CuO gears-like architectures modified Cu substrate were recently developed with a specific capacitance of 348 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 [20]. Additionally, Zhang et al. investigated a simple chemical precipitation method to prepare flower-like CuO with a reasonable specific capacitance in KOH electrolyte [21].

In order to further improve the energy storage performance of these electrodes to meet the prerequisites of real practical use, combining oxides with conductive frameworks such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, porous carbon, and etc. can increase the conductivity and surface area. GQDs with ultra-small sizes and novel physiochemical properties demonstrated a high importance for various uses including fuel cells, catalysts, batteries and photovoltaic devices. Compared to multi-dimensional carbon materials, GQDs always show a high flexibility to fabricate diverse nanoarchitectures. GQDs with variable surface functional groups such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl can sufficiently contribute to the ion-/electron-transfer process owing to their robust affinities and available active sites. In addition, nanosized GQDs possess a large surface area, elucidating an intensive interface between electrolyte and active material [22,23].

With these considerations, GQDs are thus capable of boosting the capacitive properties of electrodes for supercapacitors. Recently, Jia and co-worker reported the heterostructural GQDs/MnO2 electrode with superior capacitive properties. It was found that the assembled GQDs onto MnO2 nanosheets via Mn―O―C bonds were able to form heterostructures between components and promoted the cycling performance of the resulting composite. Such favorable heterostructures extended the potential window and tuned the specific capacity of electrode [24].

Robust metal–organic frameworks (MOF) and their complexes have witnessed an enormous growth in various potential areas of research, especially those related to energy storage and generation because of their versatile functionalities such as large surface area and thermal stability [25-27]. Such These materials are composed of metallic ions connected to organic ligands and certified to be valid structural precursors to establish metal oxides with organized porous nanoarchitectures and inherited morphologies through the thermal decomposition of corresponding MOFs.

Here, we report a MOF-derived hierarchical structure of GQDs/CuO composite and its performance in supercapacitor applications. As a structure precursor, the Cu-organic framework constituted by a coordination network of metal ions and organic ligands is constructed with the assistance of an easy solvent evaporation procedure and then converted to CuO via the subsequent thermolysis of organic ligands in air. In the resultant structure, functionalized GQDs are encapsulated further inside by using a one-step hydrothermal reaction. This may afford a vast chance and hold a potential to modify the physical/chemical features. The incorporated GQDs not only act as conductive bridges to promote the ions/electrons transportation but also supply an additional surface area with numerous active centers. Benefiting from the large specific surface area, intrinsic conductivity to favor the rapid charge transport, and hierarchical architectures, the composite displays good capacitive performances including a high capacitance and a good rate capability together with the distinguished stability during prolonged cyclic measurements. In virtue of its particular compositional and structural merits, the fabricated ASC device features high energy storage properties.

Cu-BTC powder is prepared by dissolving 640 mg Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 300 mg trimesic acid (1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid) in 10 mL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) under magnetic stirring at room temperature to produce a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, the DMSO solution is evaporated by heating the reaction in an oven at 90 °C. The formed yield is collected, rinsed with methanol, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. CuO powder with a black color can be obtained after thermal decomposition of the Cu-BTC precursors at 500 °C for 2 h under a temperature ramp of 5 °C min−1. To construct GQDs/CuO hybrid, the resultant CuO powder (100 mg) is homogeneously mixed with GQDs suspension (25 mL, 1 mg mL−1) under the magnetic stirring for 3 h at room temperature. Afterward, the reaction mixture undergoes a simple hydrothermal treatment at 150 °C for 4 h. Finally, the harvested product is dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven before further use. For comparison, CuO powder is subjected to a similar hydrothermal reaction without GQDs and denotes as hydrothermally treated CuO.
Characterization of samples and physiochemical analyses are provided in Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of electrodes and asymmetric supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of electrodes for capacitive behavior testing and design of an ASC device are performed according to literature [28,29]. Please see Supporting information for more details.

The formation of GQDs/CuO composite for asymmetric supercapacitors is accomplished through the thermal decomposition of the Cu-BTC precursor followed by a hydrothermal treatment (Fig. S1). The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) pattern in Fig. 1 a displays the transformation of Cu-BTC to CuO with a thermal treatment in an atmospheric air. It can be noticed that the weight loss of ~27.2% at ~348 °C originates from the removal of residual DMSO solvent trapped into the Cu-BTC pores and partial oxidation of organic platforms. In addition, the predominant weight loss (~68.5%) observed within the range from 350 to 450 °C results from the additional degradation of BTC ligands and subsequent decomposition to CuO [30]. No obvious thermal events occur beyond this temperature indicating that the formation of CuO takes place around 450 °C. Therefore, the pyrolysis of the as-prepared Cu-MOF precursor can be carried out at 500 °C to attain crystalline CuO phase [31].

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of the Cu-MOF precursor is depicted in Fig. S2 which clearly demonstrates the high crystalline nature and the corresponding reflections are in accordance with the previously presented literature [32]. The XRD diffraction peaks of CuO sample (Fig. 1b) can be well assigned to the standard monoclinic phase of CuO (JCPDS card No. 80-1917) [33]. After inserting GQDs, the resultant GQDs/CuO hybrid reveals similar diffraction peaks originating from CuO, suggesting a well-maintained microstructure. Moreover, the diffraction peak at ~25.5° corresponds to the incorporated GQDs. The observed sharp characteristic peaks certify the good crystallinity of samples.

As shown in Fig. S3a-b, highly interconnected/aggregated particulate-like structure is apparent throughout the Cu-BTC framework. The sample consists mostly of bulk particles with an irregular structure. After a thermal treatment, the resulting agglomerates of CuO look slightly different and become better separated with a non-uniform morphology (Fig. 2 a-c). The bulk particles of precursor begin to etch and self-organize into smaller aggregated nanoparticles (NPs) due to shrinking process during subsequent thermolysis in air. In case of GQDs/CuO composite, the aggregated NPs with a noticeable change in size become increasingly separated from each other and some spheres constituted by the primary NPs can be observed, which may possibly ascribe to the additional crystallization with a hydrothermal reaction (Fig. 2d–f). The surfaces are rough as a result of GQDs incorporation. Numerous cavities are formed due to a certain degree of the structural reorganization and etching during hydrothermal process.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and related energy dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) of the GQDs/CuO composite are illustrated in Fig. 3 a-c and S4a-b. NPs bridging nanospheres can be clearly seen throughout the TEM images (Fig. S4a). It is visible that the NPs are arranged in a disordered manner with non-uniform morphologies and assembled together to construct porous architectures (Fig. 3a-c). The formed nanospheres with a size varies from 80 to 600 nm are composed of primary particles stacked one on another (Fig. 3c and Fig. S4b). Such morphology may possibly be caused by the combination of GQDs and structural reorganization during hydrothermal reaction. These observations elucidate that the hydrothermal reaction can afford a suitable environment for particle growth and thus a hierarchical structure can be obtained. Results show that GQDs can coated the layers of CuO NPs through interfacial interactions between them (Fig. S5). The analyzed lattice spacings of 0.36 and 0.27 nm are due to (3 1 −1) and (1 1 0) planes of CuO (pointed out by light blue lines), respectively. Meanwhile the latter with 0.20 nm is assigned to (1 0 0) plane of graphene (marked by yellow lines) [34], revealing the combination of GQDs with CuO.

Elements present in the GQDs/CuO composite are probed by EDS analyses interlinked with TEM as presented in Fig. 3d. The elements are provided with different colored dots and their corresponding mapping profiles. The presence of C confirms the successful integration GQDs in the resulting composite, while the Cu and O elements are correlated to CuO. As shown, the elements are distributed uniformly inside the composite.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements are employed to examine the chemical bonding and surface elemental configuration of the as-prepared GQDs/CuO composite. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the existence of O (O 1s), C (C 1s), Cu (Cu 2p3/2, Cu 2p1/2, Cu 3p, and Cu 3s) elements in the survey scan manifests a hybrid complex. The atomic percentages of Cu, O and C elements are estimated to be 45.8%, 40.1%, and 14.1%, respectively. Deconvolution of a high-resolution spectrum is performed to get additional information about chemical bonding.

The Cu 2p core-level spectrum depicted in Fig. 4b reveals two distinct peaks at the binding energies of about ~932.1 and 952.2 eV attributed to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 of Cu+species on the near surface, respectively [35]. On the one hand, the fitted peaks at 933.9 and 953.9 eV can be identified as Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 of Cu2+ species, respectively [36]. Moreover, the shake-up satellite peaks (denote as Sat.) can be clearly seen at 941, 943.7, and 962.2 eV. The C 1s scan (Fig. 4c) can be decomposed into four characteristic forms at 284.4, 286.3, 288.1, and 292.1 eV, which are assigned to C—C, C—OH, CO, and OC—O modes, respectively [34,37]. Fig. 4d presents O1s spectrum with three adjusted peaks at ~529.5, 531.4, and 532 eV assigned to lattice oxygen (Cu–O), oxygen atoms in surface hydroxyl groups (-OH), and carbonyl oxygen (CO and/or O–CO bond to GQDs), respectively [38]. The abundant oxygen-containing groups are beneficial for Faradic redox reactions because of the robust contact with electrolyte ions.

The specific surface area and porous property of samples are calculated and presented in Fig. S6a-d. The collected N2 adsorption–desorption loops of both CuO and GQDs/CuO materials display a typical type IV isotherm with a decreasing slope at the low relative pressure region and a feasible hysteresis within the pressure range from 0.4 to 1 (P/Po) (Fig. S6a and c). The specific surface area of the GQDs/CuO composite (101.2 m2 g−1) is larger than that of pure CuO (~55.6 m2 g−1) (Fig. S6a and c). Optimized pore size distribution profiles of samples indicate their mesoporous characteristics (Fig. S6b and d). The mesopore size of the GQDs/CuO composite varies between 5 and 21 nm while pure CuO has a mesopore size centered at 6 and 9.1 nm. The mesoporous architecture inherited from MOF precursor can favor the rapid diffusion of ions and enable free pore spaces to alleviate the internal stress induced by volume change during charging-discharging processes. Furthermore, the surface area of CuO with GQDs reinforcement enlarges to nearly twice of the bald CuO, thus resulting in abundant active sites and an expanded contact at interfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of the GQDS/CuO composite
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical behaviors of the GQDs/CuO-based electrode are evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques with a three-electrode configuration. Fig. 5 a illustrates the CV graphs of pure CuO and GQDs/CuO composite in 3 M KOH solution at a constant scan rate of 10 mV s−1. In a control experiment, the electrochemical properties of hydrothermally treated CuO are then evaluated under similar conditions. It is apparent that the enclosed area of GQDs/CuO electrode is larger than those obtained by pure CuO and hydrothermally treated CuO, suggesting an intrinsically enhanced electrochemical activity. Thus, the successful implanting of GQDs results in an enlarged integral surface area, improved electrochemical redox activity, and thereby a better pseudocapacitive property.

What's more, CV signals of all electrode display oxidation and reduction characteristics directed by the reversible Faradaic redox reactions of Cu-based oxide/hydroxide species in an alkaline electrolyte [39,40]. The existence of anodic and cathodic peaks within the working potential window is mainly due to Cu(I)/Cu(II) transition and can be controlled by OH— ions. These peaks clearly manifest that the charge storage mechanism of electrodes may be realized by their pseudocapacitance nature Eq. (1)-((3)) [13].
(1) 2OH– + Cu2O ↔ 2e– + 2CuO + H2O
(2) OH– + CuOH ↔ e – + CuO + H2O
(3) OH– + CuOH ↔ e – + Cu(OH)2

The typical CV curves of GQDs/CuO electrode at various scan rates are presented in Fig. 5b. All the CV responses declare well-defined redox peaks over the entire range of sweep rate. Upon the increase of scan rate from 5 to 200 mVs−1, the peak current density increases owing to the enhanced electric conductivity of the composite. The enclosed area of the CV signals is gradually increase while maintaining a similar shape with the increase of scan rate, revealing the better electrochemical reversibility. In addition, the redox peak potential shifts toward positive and negative directions because of the internal resistance of electrode. This validates the quasi-reversible features of redox reactions and occurrence of a diffusion-controlled process.

The electrochemical performances are further investigated by GCD curves as presented in Fig. 5c and Fig. S7a-b. The nonlinear behavior and nearly symmetrical shapes of charge–discharge curves at all tested current densities confirm the pseudocapacitive nature and improved reversibility, consistent with the CV results. With reference to Fig. 5c, it can be found that the discharge time of the GQDs/CuO electrode is the largest among the three different electrodes. Accordingly, the specific capacitance of pure CuO, hydrothermally treated CuO, and GQDs/CuO electrodes at a current density of 1 A g−1 are 413, 448 and 729 F g−1, respectively. GCD curves of pure CuO, hydrothermally treated CuO, and GQDs/CuO are tested at varying current densities as illustrated in Fig. 5d and Fig. S7.

Moreover, the relationship between specific capacity and discharge current density is depicted in Fig. 5e. Over the whole current density range, GQDs/CuO composite shows a higher specific capacitance than those of pure CuO and hydrothermally treated CuO. The GQDs/CuO electrode exhibits a good rate capability of 58% as the current density rises from 1 to 50 A g−1, which is higher than those of pure CuO (47.2%) and hydrothermally treated CuO (55.3%). This may be attributed to the accelerated transport rate of electrons during charge-discharge cycles guided by the synergistic effect between the CuO and GQDs [31]. Apparently, the gradual decrease in specific capacitance with increasing current density may be associated with the incremental IR drop and insufficient active material involved in Faradic redox reactions [41]. Thus, the proposed GQDs/CuO composite delivers better capacitive performances compared with previously reported CuO-based materials (Table S1). It is worthy mentioned that either CV or GCD data evidence the enhanced capacitive performances of GQDs/CuO composite, which are largely attributed to the modified electric conductivity as a result of GQDs reinforcements and construction of large pores and cavities with a rapid mass transfer, giving the abundant exposure of active sites and redox species.

Cyclic performance of pure CuO, hydrothermally treated CuO, and GQDs/CuO-based electrodes is evaluated by repeated GCD experiments for 3000 cycles at 10 A g−1 (Fig. 5f). It can be seen that the specific capacitance of pure CuO and hydrothermally treated CuO keeps about 70.6% and 74.7%, respectively of its initial value after 3000 cycles. In a sharp contrast, the GQDs/CuO electrode reveals a higher cyclic stability with a capacitance retention rate of 82.2%. The improved cycling performance of the composite correlates to the hierarchical architecture with an intimate contact between components. We further probe the microstructure and surface morphology of the GQDs/CuO composite after long-time cycling. The SEM analysis almost maintains the original morphology after repeated charge-discharge processes (Fig. S8a-b). In addition, XRD pattern is recorded to check the crystalline phase of the GQDs/CuO composite after durability test. As represented in Fig. S9, the sample retains its original phase, revealing the excellent structural and compositional stability. The desired conductivity and large surface area of the composite can facilitate a stable electron/ion transport during the reversible redox reactions.

The in-depth charge transfer kinetics at boundaries and ion diffusion can be explored via EIS measurements with the frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The acquired Nyquist plots of electrodes display a semi-circular loop within the high-frequency region and a vertical line in the low-frequency region (Fig. S10). These plots are fitted using the proposed equivalent circuit model (inset-Fig. S10). The proposed equivalent circuit model has an electrolyte resistance (R s), a charge-transfer resistance (R ct), a constant phase element (CPE) reflects the pseudocapacitance induced by the redox process of active material and double layer capacitance, and the Warburg impedance (R w) [42]. It is clear that the GQDs/CuO electrode has a smaller R ct value of (5.62 Ω) compared with that of pure CuO (8.94 Ω), confirming the favorable charge transfer and faster reaction kinetics of the composite. Moreover, the R s of GQDs/CuO (0.36 Ω) is smaller than that of pure CuO (0.43 Ω) owing to the hierarchical architectures and GQDs modification, which positively contribute to the electrical transport. The loose structure can facilitate the effective permeation of ions to exposed surfaces and promote the Faradaic charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capacitive performance of GQDS/CuO//AC ASC device
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we build up an ASC device to further check the performance of proposed GQDs/CuO for practical use and measure the energy and power densities by constructing an ASC system. The GQDs/CuO composite (positive electrode) acts as the energy source while the AC (negative electrode) serves as the power source. The mass loading ratio of the negative to positive electrode is optimized according to the charge balance relationship (Fig. 6 a and Fig. S11) [28]. By integrating AC and GQDs/CuO composite in an ASC cell, a full use of the working voltage can be realized. Therefore, the operating potential of the as-fabricated GQDs/CuO//AC ASC device can reach 1.5 V based on the stable working voltages of both materials with a three-electrode configuration (Fig. 6a).

The CV and GCD measurements of the assembled GQDs/CuO//AC ASC device in 3 M KOH electrolyte with the potential window of 0 to 1.5 V are given in Fig. 6b-c. CV profiles of the ASC device at various scanning rates from 5 to 200 mVs−1 demonstrate that both GQDs/CuO composite and AC contribute to the entire capacitance of integrated device. The obtained profiles suggest a dual collaboration of a pseudocapacitance from the GQDs/CuO composite and an electric double-layer capacitive characteristic of conventional AC (Fig. 6b). GCD results at different current densities from 1 to 15 A g−1 are provided in Fig. 6c. The non-standard triangular shapes of GCD profiles are in accordance with the CV results.

The specific capacitances of the device derived from the discharge part of GCD curves are summarized in Fig. 6d. The device can deliver a high capacitance of 103 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and still keeps a specific capacitance of 52.8 F g−1 even at a higher current density of 15 A g−1 together with a favorable rate capability of ~ 51.2%, confirming the enhanced electrochemical performance. Upon the increase of current density, the limitation of diffusion effect at higher current densities may reduce the utilization rate of active material and Faradic processes at electrode surfaces. In addition, the variation in rates of ion exchange of a pseudocapacitive material and carbon-based electrode material may possibly induce imbalanced charges between them [28].

Ragone plot of the constructed ASC device is analyzed as depicted in Fig. 6e. The GQDs/CuO//AC ASC device achieves a maximum energy density of 32.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 748.9 W kg−1. Even at a high-power density of 11 250 W kg−1, the device can still preserve a large energy density 16.5 Wh kg−1, indicating an enhanced energy density. The satisfactory energy/power values of the GQDs/CuO//AC apparatus are more comparable or even better than previously reported systems such as NiCo2S4@PPy/NF//AC (34.62 Wh kg−1 at 120.19 W kg−1) [43], NiCo2O4//RGO@Fe3O4 (25 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1) [44], CKF/CoNiOx//AC (23.2 Wh kg−1 at 200 W kg−1) [45], Ni(OH)2//AC (35.7 Wh kg−1 at 490 W kg−1) [46], CuO@rGO@SP//rGO@SP (31.2 Wh kg−1 at 1699 W kg−1) [47], CuO//AC (19.7 Wh kg−1 at 700 W kg−1) [40], Ni-MOF/rGO-300//AC (17.13 Wh kg−1 at 750 W kg−1) [42], and CVO Cu@CuO//AC (35.43 Wh kg−1 at 520.99 W kg−1) [48]. Table S1 compares the energy storage performances of the GQDs/CuO//AC ASC device with similar reported cells.

Next, long cycling life of the GQDs/CuO//AC ASC device is assessed by GCD. The test is conducted at a high current density of 10 A g−1 for 8000 successive cycles (Fig. 6f). As the number of cycles increases, the active sites exposure and utilization efficiency of electrode materials declines, leading to a gradual degradation of the specific capacity. The device shows a stable decay and realizes a high retention rate and keeps around 86.3% of its initial capacitance until 8000 cycles, implying a good structural integrity and cycling stability performance.

To validate the good cyclic stability of the ASC device, EIS measurements are conducted and the related Nyquist plots before and after long-term cycling are illustrated in Fig. S12. It can be observed that the R s value almost unchanged, which certifies the enhanced diffusion of ions and good electrochemical activity. More important, the R ct of the device increases slightly from 9.14 Ω to 9.84 Ω after 8000 cycles, which may possibly attribute to the minor alterations in local ion concentration during cyclic test [39]. Overall, the EIS results confirm the improved Faradaic redox reactions and lower diffusion resistance, which are favorable for the sustained cycling performance and high-rate capability of electrode.

There is no doubt that the enhancements in electrochemical performance are strongly associated with the inherent compositional and architectural aspects of the GQDs/CuO composite, whereby: (i) besides CuO species that donate to a pseudocapacitance, the embedment of GQDs can further afford more electrochemical active sites to accelerate the ion accessibility and accumulation of charges. Their tunable electronic conductivity can reduce the resistance at interface and modulate the electron transportation, thereby enabling a better performance with a higher capacitance, (ii) the hydrophilic carboxyl groups of GQDs may allow good ions affinity to the electrode configuration because of their higher polarity. As a consequence, the electrolyte ions diffusion and accessibility as well as the kinetics of redox reactions can be boosted, (iii) the large surface area and favourable porosity may provide a sufficient surface exposure with numerous active sites for effective redox reactions at electrode/electrolyte interfaces. These features possess a synergistic role contributing to the electrochemical performance.

In summary, a hybrid structured GQDs/CuO is prepared by an easy solvent evaporation route followed by a hydrothermal reaction for supercapacitors. CuO aggregates are synthesized using a Cu-MOF complex as the structural and compositional precursor, while GQDs firmly anchor the CuO layers as an external support. Specifically, pseudocapacitive Cu species can control electrochemical activity and facilitate the Faradic redox reactions, meanwhile immobilizing functionalized GQDs firmly on the surface of CuO is supposed to provide more active sites, enhance electric conductivity, and promote ion accessibility. As a cathodic material in supercapacitors, the GQDs/CuO electrode exhibits a high specific capacitance (729 F g−1 at 1 A g−1), good rate capability (58% capacitance retention at 50 A g−1), and an improved cycling stability. Meanwhile, the as-assembled GQDs/CuO//AC ASC apparatus delivers a maximum energy density of 32.2 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 748.9 W kg−1 together with a good cycle life (86.3% capacitance retention after 8000 cycles). The improved electrochemical properties of the GQDs/CuO composite are strongly correlated to its compositional and architectural features. Appropriate porosity can attenuate the structural expansion during repeated cycling. Moreover, incorporating GQDs further adds more specific surface areas, increase the electronic conductivity of CuO, and thereby plentiful exposed active sites as well as rapid transportation processes of electrons within the electrode can be attained. The obtained results may prompt the development and applications energy storage systems.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key Research and Development Program (Grant No. 2016YFB0901600), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. LY19E020014, and NSFC (Grant No. 21303162 and Grant No. 11604295).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2021.04.037.
